reasoning: The finding is confirmed real. At lines 297 and 314 of handle_captcha(), the POST parameter g-recaptcha-response is processed only by sanitize_text_field() before being concatenated into the wp_remote_get URL. sanitize_text_field() strips HTML tags and percent-encoded sequences but does NOT encode or strip ampersand (&) characters. An attacker can submit g-recaptcha-response=foo&secret=ATTACKER_SECRET and the resulting verification URL becomes https://www.google.com/recaptcha/api/siteverify?secret=SITE_SECRET&response=foo&secret=ATTACKER_SECRET. wp_remote_get passes the URL string raw to curl without re-encoding the query string. The reachability is correct: this is triggered by any unauthenticated user submitting a login or registration form with reCAPTCHA enabled. The severity is low/medium rather than critical because exploitation requires: (1) Google's API using the last duplicate secret= parameter value, and (2) the attacker submitting a valid reCAPTCHA token generated against their own account. If both conditions hold, an attacker can bypass reCAPTCHA verification by injecting their own secret key and submitting a self-generated token — effectively bypassing bot protection. The code has no URL encoding of the response value before concatenation, no use of urlencode/rawurlencode/http_build_query, and no guard against metacharacters. The vulnerability class (url_parameter_injection) is correct. Severity is low because exploitation has external dependencies (Google's duplicate param handling) and does not grant server access, only bypasses captcha.
